I literally just came out the theatre after watching the movie and it was so good I had to write this  immediately.This movie is an absolute masterpiece! The way they portrayed everything so perfectly it was so clever and relatable at the same time!! (Spoiler alert) I absolutely LOVE how they portrayed brainstorming ideas as an ACTUAL storm of ideas and how anxiety suppressing the other emotions and how she literally froze when Riley was having a panic attack, I mean that was absolutely genius. I am about to be an adult now and in my initial teen years, this was EXACTLY how I felt, the anxiety, embarrassment, envy, sarcasm, stress, confusion, boredom, EVERYTHING ALL AT ONCE. I am still dealing with anxiety and other mental health issues and I have to say this movie definitely portrays what we as human don't know how to say. Would definitely recommend to watch this one, another masterpiece by Pixar

Anxiety is an unpredictable emotion for humans such as us depending on a type of situation they might experience in the past, present, or future. However, it backfires without having proper discipline on catching up with other responsibilities at the same time affecting her self-esteem thanks to Embarassment and Ennui. This could lead Riley to hit rock-bottom on struggling to make the right decisions to salvage her relationship with society before hanging out with the wrong people or committing bad habits (self harm). Amy Poehler accepted an offer of $5 million with lucrative bonuses to reprise her role as Joy. Phyllis Smith and Lewis Black also reprise their roles as Sadness and Anger, respectively. Following a dispute over pay, both Mindy Kaling and Bill Hader declined to reprise their respective roles as Disgust and Fear; they and the rest of the returning cast were reportedly offered $100,000 each, equivalent to two percent of Poehler's salary. On November 9, 2023, with the release of the teaser trailer, it was revealed that Tony Hale and Liza Lapira would replace Hader and Kaling as Fear and Disgust, respectively, while Maya Hawke joined the cast as Anxiety, a new emotion. Kelsey Mann auditioned Maya Hawke via Zoom at the office of a back room in Epcot during a family vacation with his kids after Mark Nielsen told him that Hawke was available to audition just then, with her anxious performance driving him to tears.

Not exactly excited with this movie because it breaks continuity. There's a scene in the original Inside Out during the credits where we explore the minds of dogs, elders, teenagers, and babies, and see that all of these age groups have 5 core emotions.

Riley manifesting new emotions like this is a reflective fault on the first film, where the emotions should've never had balance in their roles and given Joy the reigns. 

What's with the continuity breaking plots recently anyway? First we had forky who absolutely wrecked the requirements for what's considered a toy, then Kung-fu Panda 4 forgets about Mantis and Snake, and finally established universal laws are bent in order for temporary emotions to take centre-stage for a puberty PSA.

Clearly there wasn't supposed to be a sequel because disgust was always a teenage snob and fear a balding middle-aged man. All of these new emotions look like teenagers, and if this film has taken 8 years to come to fruition, then I believe some Pixar higher-up demanded a sequel to a high grossing movie because *money ka-ching*

I know that technically fear is a reaction to a perceived threat and anxiety is worrying about a threat that hasn’t or may never happen yet, but that’s also how Fear acted in the first movie. It just seems weird having two characters with basically the same personalities. But whatever, that probably won’t affect the movie in a long term and I’m sure it will still be relatively good.

So some thoughts on this...

- Joy is responsible for keeping Riley happy
- Sadness signals that Riley needs comforting when upset or vulnerable
- Anger gets Riley to fight her corner when treated unfairly
- Disgust keeps Riley from accepting anything that is (broadly speaking) offensive to her
- Fear is to keep Riley safe from harm

But if these five are 'bottled up' that means Riley will become joyless, unwilling to get help when upset, vulnerable to unfair treatment, more accepting of offensiveness and reckless in the face of danger.  

And while they are bottled up the new four emotions will be doing their thing...

- Anxiety will cause Riley to obsess with potential, conceptual and abstract threats to her reputation, credibility and space to maneuverer in social situations stressing her out

- Embarrassment will cause Riley to become awkward when she makes inevitable mistakes in her life, perhaps caused by the stress of Anxiety?

- Envy will drive Riley crazy with jealousy at what others have and she doesn't have (or believes she can't have or can no longer have), perhaps as a result of Embarrassment making her too self-conscious to even try anymore leading to resentfulness?

- Ennui will bring about listlessness in Riley making her uninterested in her responsibilities and anything that would require an effort on her part, perhaps a result of Envy's work leading her to think why bother trying if it will lead to failure and awkwardness?

And then full circle her boredom and inactivity leads to further problems (both real and imagined), caused by Anxiety?

Similar to the original 5 emotions, I love how the new ones also loosely resemble something in line with what they are.

Anxiety resembles an explosion, or that of an erupting volcano—harkening to "bursts" of panic we experience when anxious

Envy, with her sparkling eyes and turquoise palette, resembles a diamond, or any similar precious jewel—something usually sought after in media, which is quite ironic considering her entire character revolves around seeking something she does not have.

Embarrassment, with his large pink form, resembles a blushing cheek, a response we get when we feel embarrassed. Additionally, he also resembles a pimple—another embarrassing mark—especially when he closes his hoodie.

As for Ennui, I'm rather unsure of what she represents, other than being yarn or wet noodles (something similar to her limp demeanor); but if I have to speculate, I'd like to imagine that she resembles a wilted flower—something once full of happiness and energy, now drained of all life and in a state of listlessness, as shown with a limp stem and drooping petals (her hair).

That's it; thank you.

Im curious as to how exactly it will play a role in rileys life as its clear for the most part where Embarassment, anxiety, and envy fit in. Riley is worried about embarassing herself in front of the 'cool kids,' shes anxious of life and the changes that come with it. Envious of what the cool kids have that she doesnt whether its materialistic or the confidence in how they present themselves, While shes a nervous socially awkward individual. I dont immediately see where and how Ennui will play a role as we dont see her do anything besides lay on the couch staring at a phone. She never seemingly interacts with the control panel except once off screen. Every other time shes on screen, shes just staring at her phone. So I really hope Ennui isnt just a one note character that contributes nothing of substance. I really hope she has a bigger role than what we are seeing.

Inside Out 1 came out when I was an 11-12 year old with undiagnosed autism, and it really hit me like a bus. I loved it so much, I really connected to it. I can't wait for this to come out now that I'm 20.

I think it's really heartwarming that Joy has fully accepted Sadness for who she is, and how important she is for Riley. And I like already how Fear and Anxiety are fearful/worried about two completely different things: Fear is worried for more concrete concerns in the present, anxiety is worried about more abstract concerns that could or could not be in the future. Fear is worried about the possibility that Riley could get hit in the mouth with a hockey puck (which is rational and easily fixed), Anxiety is worried that if Riley does not make a good first impression on the cool kids that in the future she will not be popular/have friends (which is irrational but feels oh so important now that she has to or else she will be thinking about it for the next year).

Inside Out is one of those Disney movies where a sequel actually makes sense and could work very well.

All the emotions are physically based off of something: Joy is a beam of sunlight, Sadness is a tear drop, Fear is a nerve ending, Disgust is a stalk of Broccoli and Anger is a magma rock. 
First impressions for the new emotions are Anxiety is a carrot or similar root vegetable, Ennui is a raven and Embarrassment is fittingly enough a pimple. I can't really guess what Envy is. Maybe a blueberry?

Anxiety and Embarrassment just fall into Fear, and Envy and Boredom just fall into Disgust.
Other question, why don’t adults have these new emotions? As independent, self-providing adults and parents, they would need them even more than teens.
Unless adults “bottle them up” after teenage years (which doesn’t make sense because even if they don’t show it as it is bottled up, they still feel those emotions, similar to how if you restrained yourself from crying, you would still have Sadness) or they disappear as the person learns to control them (which doesn’t make sense; a patient person would still have Anger at HQ). Additionally, why didn’t the dog at the end of the first movie have Envy for the food? Why didn’t Riley have Embarrassment when she cried in front of the class?
But overall, I do see the coming-of-age themes here. They do seem somewhat cleverly put together.

You know, lately I've been sick of all the sequels and remakes Disney's been coming out with the past few years, but honestly? This one actually looks pretty good! I wouldn't say Inside Out is one of my favorite movies, but it is pretty special to me if only because it is the only movie that makes me cry literally every single time I watch it lol. Out of Riley’s 4 new emotions, which one would you say you felt the most of pushing through high school?

Anxiety - where events here and there made you feel uneasy or on edge.

Envy - where seeing people and the things that they had made you wish you could have that same treatment.

Ennui - where you practically felt the experience unamusing and uninteresting.

Embarrassment- where you’ve experienced moments that make your self-esteem get crushed like an ant.

I think one of the coolest part about this film being released almost 10 years after the first film, is that the children that grew up seeing this movie are likely going through what Riley is in this film.
Puberty.
Several therapists have attested to how Inside Out helped young children understand and process their emotions, and now those children are growing up and developing feelings they have even less understanding of. My parents kind of glossed over having any of these discussions and didn't address them until they became problematic, so I hope adults and even therapists see this movie to help their young pre-teens/teens navigate the misery of puberty.

I think what worries me most about the suppressed emotions bit is, if you're actively suppressing joy, and all you have to work with is anxiety, envy, boredom, and embarrassment? Doesn't seem like it would be possible for you to be happy. None of those are really rooted in contentment. I spend a lot of time being bored, I would say that's probably the most positive of the new emotion batch, which feels weird to say, but I think it's true. Some of my worst high school memories involve anxiety, envy, or embarrassment, but boredom was more like, "Meh, I don't care THAT much" so it helped me move on quicker. Only trouble was eventually getting off that couch... and she looked might comfy, lounging in those socks. Notice how little she was featured, which just figures.

I think one of the coolest part about this film being released almost 10 years after the first film, is that the children that grew up seeing this movie are likely going through what Riley is in this film.
Puberty.
Several therapists have attested to how Inside Out helped young children understand and process their emotions, and now those children are growing up and developing feelings they have even less understanding of. My parents kind of glossed over having any of these discussions and didn't address them until they became problematic, so I hope adults and even therapists see this movie to help their young pre-teens/teens navigate the misery of puberty.

Emotions are the vibrant hues painting the canvas of human experience. From the euphoric highs of joy to the profound depths of sorrow, they shape our perceptions and actions. Love envelops us in warmth, while anger ignites like wildfire. Fear tightens its grip, while hope whispers promises of better days. Sadness washes over like a heavy rain, cleansing the soul of pain. Emotions connect us, binding hearts in empathy or tearing them apart in conflict. They dance within us, orchestrating the symphony of our existence, reminding us that to feel deeply is to be undeniably alive, navigating the labyrinth of the human condition.

Fun Fact: The emotions colors for this movie remind me of UNO flip.

The old emotions's colors seem to largely resemble the colors from the original UNO deck
Red: Anger
Yellow: Joy
Green: Disgust
Blue: Sadness
Black Special Cards: Fear (since that's the closest thing I could find)

The new emotions resemble the colors of the flipped UNO deck
Orange: Anxiety
Turquoise: Envy
Purple: Ennui
Pink: Embarrassment

Fun Fact: The emotions colors for this movie remind me of UNO flip.

The old emotions's colors seem to largely resemble the colors from the original UNO deck
Red: Anger
Yellow: Joy
Green: Disgust
Blue: Sadness
Black Special Cards: Fear (since that's the closest thing I could find)

The new emotions resemble the colors of the flipped UNO deck
Orange: Anxiety
Turquoise: Envy
Purple: Ennui
Pink: Embarrassment

The trailer looks good! Considering how downhill Disney is going (live action remakes, Wish being so bland it got blamed for being AI written, etc), I didn't react positively when I heard they're making a sequel. Same with Zootropolis, I love that show and several years ago I would've done a lot to see more of the characters, but considering the state of Disney, a sequel is that last thing I want considering they may seriously ruin it.

Similarly, when I learned they were having Anxiety felt random, considering Fear literally already covered that. Tbf, I was willing to accept Anxiety, because Riley is growing up and I can see potential for a relatability and comedy in adding Anxiety, as a teen (albeit now a mid-to-late teen, in contrast to Riley who is now apparently 13 in the sequel). From this trailer, it got even worse for a second, because it implies Riley didn't have embarrassment or boredom during her childhood? Or they were covered by Fear/the other emotions, but are concentrating as new emotions during her teen hood now?

But as I was watching the trailer just now, darn does it look good. Maybe because it's not actually that good but because I was expecting far worse, or everything else is worse, it seems good? Or perhaps it genuinely is good? But darn, that trailer makes me actually want to watch it.

Anxiety: Hello! I'm Anxiety. Where can I put my stuff? 

Joy: A new emotion.

Anxiety: I'm sorry. We wanted to make such a good first impression.

Disgust: What do you mean "we"?

Envy: I'm Envy. Look at your hair!

Disgust: Oh, yeah, not happening. 

Anxiety: That's Ennui. 

Joy: En-what?

Ennui: It's what you would call the boredom.

Sadness: What's your name big fella?

Anxiety: That's Embarrassment. 

Joy: Welcome to headquarters, Embarrassment! Oh, we're doing a fist-- No, no, oh, no. Go high? Oh, you got a real sweaty palm there, buddy.

Riley Andersen is 13 like Mei Mei from Turning Red.

So how is it that Riley has the exact same emotions as her parents when she was a younger kid? That and since when do adults and kids not have the emotions of stress, embarrassment, and ennui? That isn’t only exclusive to teenagers. They said emotions like stress are more complex and sophisticated and that they belong with a teen, but apparently not an adult like the mom was shown in trailer? Furthermore, since when are feelings of sadness, envy, and happiness too basic and simple for teenagers that they get entirely suppressed, that makes no sense.

   This trailer while interesting, makes no sense on a fundamental level of how one’s brain would go about handling emotions. At least the first movie had some logic and grounding to its high fantasy concept of the brain and emotions. If anything this trailer shows the second movie has a very inaccurate understanding of how emotions work for each individual, but I guess it makes for a cute joke about “suppressed emotions” being literally in a bottle.

I’m a little anxious about how they might handle the concept of anxiety itself (I know the silliness of this isn’t lost on me). 

I have a few thoughts on why this is exactly but I’m diagnosed autistic and can have a hard time translating those thoughts into at least semi coherent words.

It’s rare for me to be excited about an upcoming movie now days but I am very hopeful about this movie and am genuinely excited to see it when it comes out.

Especially because I originally thought the first was just ok when I saw it in theaters at age 14 almost 15. 

But then I grew up and was lucky enough to go to intensive outpatient therapy using CBT methods/understand it as more than “girl moves and doesn’t like it”.

Now it’s one of my favorite movies and feel like it handles it subject matter well with subtlety and creativity.

TLDR: Actually excited for this movie because I grew to love the first so much but am a bit nervous how they will handle the subject of anxiety but so far looks promising.

Since from the looks of it when Joy responded she caused Riley to sound rather childish(?), it would be interesting if this confronted the idea of repressing emotions that we believe make us immature or annoying so that we can fit in.
I feel like this is opening a door for the potential of Inside Out becoming a series where they show the process of growing up and how it effects our emotions.

I... don't think I like this. The original movie was good, not because of the actual premise of emotions inside Riley's head, but because of how emotionally impactful it is and it's great message and it's Bing Bong. Plus, majority of the movie spent time outside of Riley's actual brain. This seems like the entire focus will be on keeping the new emotions in control, and just seems like it'll feel super awkward and unrealistic. Besides, the original movie was intended to have more emotions, but they were scrapped to prevent a too crowded cast. This seems to have that exact problem.

I'll still probably watch it, but I don't think there is any way in hell this movie matches the first Inside Out in terms of quality.
